era: Indie Rock. E=5 -- power-pop caught in the post-Nirvana major-label frenzy. Frontier to Epic. Dropped. Six years. v1.49.336.

location.formative: Seattle indie rock scene, early 1990s. Power-pop amid grunge.
location.career: Flop. 1990-1996. Frontier Records then Epic Records. Dropped after modest sales.

members.name: Rusty Willoughby
members.note: Seattle scene fixture. Post-Flop solo career. Pure Joy. Respected figure.

theory_nodes.concept: The post-Nirvana major-label frenzy -- after Nevermind went diamond, major labels signed dozens of Seattle bands. Most were dropped when they didn't sell in Nirvana numbers. The gold rush consumed its prospectors.
examples: Dozens of bands signed to majors.
Flop: Frontier (indie) to Epic (major) to dropped.
theory_nodes.description: Post-Nirvana frenzy. Major-label trap. Swept up, spit out.

genealogy.relationship: Flop at 195 is the cautionary tale of the post-Nirvana frenzy.

theory_nodes.concept: Frontier to Epic -- the indie-to-major arc. Frontier Records (respected indie, natural fit) to Epic Records (global major, scale mismatch). The polished major-label album missed what made the band appealing.
examples: The rough edges were the appeal.
theory_nodes.description: Indie to major. Natural to polished. Identity diluted.

genealogy.relationship: Flop at 195. The label trajectory tells the industry story.

theory_nodes.concept: Power-pop in the grunge shadow -- Flop played power-pop, not grunge. Closer to Fastbacks and Young Fresh Fellows. But the major-label frenzy didn't distinguish genre -- it signed everything from Seattle.
examples: Not grunge. Cleaner, brighter, more melodic.
Labels didn't care about genre -- just geography.
Signed for being from Seattle, not for their sound.
theory_nodes.description: Power-pop in grunge's shadow. Genre-blind signing frenzy.

genealogy.relationship: Flop at 195. Power-pop mistaken for grunge potential.

cross_references.relationship: Power-pop peers. Fastbacks survived on PopLlama. Flop tried the major-label route. Different paths, different outcomes.
Same indie ecosystem. Fellows stayed indie. Flop went major. The indie path preserved the band.
E=3 to E=5. Twee to power-pop. Energy rises from the twee valley.
Another band that survived by staying indie (Sub Pop). Indie loyalty as survival strategy.
